title: Throwing a milk jug
subtitle: A wheel-thrown pitcher with a pulled spout and handle
excerpt: A stoneware milk jug thrown on the wheel, with a pulled lip spout formed while the clay is still on the bat and a pulled handle attached at the leather-hard stage. The jug holds approximately 300 ml. Requires a potter's wheel and kiln access to cone 6.
sourceNotes: Jug throwing technique derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for historical jug form reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: Settling a clay mound on the wheel head so it rotates without wobbling.
Drawing clay upward between fingers to thin and raise the wall of a thrown pot.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to attach handles and trimmings.
The trimmed-back base ridge a finished pot sits on.
The first firing at cone 06 that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware.
A pyrometric measure of heat-work. Cone 06 is roughly 1000°C; cone 6 is roughly 1220°C.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.

Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio area.
A milk jug combines two throwing skills with two hand-building additions: throwing the body, forming the lip spout, pulling a handle, and attaching the handle at the
leather-hard
stage. The jug is thrown from 400 g of smooth stoneware. Kiln access to
cone
6 is required.

items.name: Smooth stoneware
items.quantity: 400 g for the body, 80 g for the handle

content.text: Throwing the body
Centre 400 g of smooth stoneware.
Centring

complete when the mound runs still under a fixed finger. Open to about 70 mm diameter. Pull the wall in three pulls to a height of about 110 mm with walls 5-6 mm thick. The jug belly should be widest at about two-thirds of its height. Compress the outside wall with the wooden rib in an upward sweeping motion to refine the form.
Forming the lip spout

While the jug is still on the wheel, form the pouring spout at the rim. Rest a finger on the inside of the rim at the point where the spout will be. Press the rim outward and upward with the finger while supporting the outside with the thumb. Draw the spout into a gentle V-shape 15-20 mm wide at the top. The spout should rise slightly higher than the rest of the rim so the pour does not drip over the edge.
Drying to leather-hard
Pulling a wall
complete, wire the jug from the bat and allow it to stiffen to leather-hard upright on a board. Cover the spout with a damp cloth for the first 2 hours so it dries at the same rate as the body.
Trimming the foot ring
At leather-hard, invert the jug and trim a
foot ring
with the loop tool. The foot ring on a jug is typically 5-6 mm wide and 8 mm tall, set 10 mm inward from the jug base edge.
Pulling and attaching the handle

Pull a handle from 80 g of clay: hold the clay in one hand and pull it downward with a wet other hand in long smooth strokes, rotating the clay slightly between each pull. The pulled handle should be about 130 mm long and 15 mm wide, tapering slightly toward the lower end. Allow the pulled handle to stiffen for 15-20 minutes on the board until it holds its shape but is still flexible.

Score both attachment points on the jug body and the handle ends with the needle tool. Brush slip onto both. Press the top end of the handle firmly below the rim at the back of the jug, opposite the spout. Curve the handle into shape and press the lower end onto the jug body about 30 mm above the foot ring. Blend each attachment point with a fingertip.
Firing
Allow the jug to reach bone-dry before loading the kiln.
Bisque
at cone 06, then glaze and fire to cone 6 for mature stoneware.
Care
A cone 6 stoneware jug with a food-safe tested glaze on all interior surfaces is suitable for milk, water, and general table use. Dishwasher-safe.
Variations
Throw a matching set of four small cream jugs from 150 g each using the off-the-hump technique.
Add a thumbrest to the handle by pressing a small knob of clay at the upper handle attachment point before it dries.
Increase the wall height to 160 mm and the starting weight to 700 g for a litre jug.

rows.problem: Handle cracks at the attachment point during drying
rows.cause: Handle was wetter than the jug body when attached, causing differential shrinkage
rows.fix: Let the pulled handle stiffen until it has the same feel as the jug body before attaching. Match moisture levels.

rows.cause: The spout lip does not extend above the rim level, so liquid flows back along the outside of the jug
rows.fix: Form the spout slightly higher than the adjacent rim. The V-shape should rise 3-4 mm above the rim edge.

rows.problem: Jug base is thicker than the walls
rows.cause: Opening was too shallow, or the base was not thinned during pulling
rows.fix: On the final pull, lightly compress the base with a fingertip from the inside to thin it toward the standard wall thickness.
